My inbox is a mess because I have always used the same email for work and personal use, and now it is too late to change that. This also means my inbox is cluttered and disorganized. It is a chaotic mix of client requests, newsletters, and automated notifications. Even Gmail’s default categories struggle to separate important messages from noise. I was able to take back some control with an email manager built using n8n. I created a workflow that monitors Gmail, analyzes each email with an AI model, and automatically applies the correct labels.

The workflow begins with a Gmail trigger node that captures new messages, gathering their content, headers, and metadata. An AI node then analyzes the message in context, checking previous interactions, thread history, and content to determine its category. Messages are labeled according to custom categories I set up: “To Respond,” “FYI,” “Notification,” “Marketing,” “Meeting Update,” and “Comment.”

I use Claude for AI analysis, but the workflow also supports other models, such as ChatGPT, Gemini, or Grok. Additional Gmail nodes fetch history from both the inbox and sent folder, while a structured output parser ensures consistent labeling. The workflow loops back to Gmail to automatically apply the labels.

Personal assistant for email, calendar, and more

Stay on top of your day

One of the most powerful automations I’ve built with n8n is what I call my personal communication assistant. It’s essentially a system that scans my entire digital workspace, including email, calendar, and Slack, and provides me with a daily rundown of what needs my attention.

I start this workflow with a Cron node scheduled for 8:00 AM every weekday. From there, a Gmail Trigger node checks my inbox and pulls in all unread messages, while a Gmail node fetches their content, headers, and thread history. These details are then passed to a Claude node, which classifies messages based on urgency and context. For instance, if an email is from someone I’ve recently interacted with, it’s flagged as high priority. If it’s a newsletter or a system alert, it’s marked as low priority and ignored for now.

The next part of the workflow focuses on meetings. A Google Calendar node retrieves events from the past three days and checks for any follow-ups I might have missed. If the meeting was recorded, a Fireflies API node fetches the transcript, and another Claude node scans it for action items or unresolved questions. Parallel to this, a Slack Trigger node monitors direct messages and mentions, while a Slack node gathers unread conversations. These are again analyzed by Claude, which decides which ones need responses and which can wait.

Once all this data is collected, a Function node acts as the orchestrator, merging insights from email, meetings, and Slack into a single structured output. This is then passed into a Google Sheets node to update my running to-do list and a Slack node to send me a daily briefing message.

Summarize YouTube videos

For when you are in a hurry

Another useful automation I have built with n8n is a YouTube summarizer. I watch a lot of long-form content for research and learning, but sometimes I don’t have the time to sit through a one-hour talk. I built a workflow that does the heavy lifting for me. All I need to do is paste a YouTube URL, and within a few minutes, I get a structured summary with main topics, key insights, and supporting details.

The workflow begins with a Webhook node, which acts as the entry point where I submit the video link. A YouTube API node then fetches details about the video, while a Transcript Extraction node automatically pulls the full transcript. It works with most public videos and handles different URL formats. Once the transcript is ready, it’s passed to a GPT-4 node, which analyzes the content, breaks it down into sections, and organizes the information into a clear, easy-to-read structure.

The final part of the workflow depends on how I want to receive the summary. A Telegram node can send the results directly to my phone, or a Google Docs node can log them into a running database of research notes.

There’s nothing you can’t automate using n8n

That’s what their website says, and I find it to be quite true. You can automate almost anything, and if you are not sure where to start, someone in the community has probably already built and documented that automation. n8n is not the only platform for no-code automations. Tines is another handy option, and now there are also AI browsers like Dia and Comet that can handle a lot of tasks for you.

n8n
See at n8n

item.title: This new Linux desktop environment gives us what Microsoft never did with Windows
item.itunes:title: This new Linux desktop environment gives us what Microsoft never did with Windows
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Since I've made the switch from Windows to Linux as my primary go-to OS on laptops, I've tried a few desktop environments, from Cinnamon, to GNOME, and eventually settled on KDE Plasma as a personal favorite. But recently, a new contender has entered the discussion, and it comes from a familiar group: System76, the team behind Pop!_OS.

The new COSMIC desktop entered beta just a couple of weeks ago, after spending over a year in alpha testing, and I decided to jump in and give it a shot. And from what I've seen so far, it's looking very promising as an alternative to the existing desktop environments. Plus, it comes with one feature that Microsoft promised Windows users for years before giving up on it.

Sets, is that you?

Your windows are tabs now

Among the many features that Windows users have lost in recent years, one of the most interesting is one we never truly got in the first place. Sets was a feature Microsoft introduced to the Windows Insider Program during the development of Windows 10 in 2017, and it essentially allowed any app to run inside a tab, so you could have multiple apps running side-by-side in the same window. However, it was removed before the launch of the next major update, only to reappear again in the Insider program with some enhancements in tow. Unfortunately, those enhancements weren't enough, and the feature was once again removed before the next major update, never to be seen again.

One of the standout features of the COSMIC desktop environment is the ability to easily stack windows into the same window, essentially replicating Sets, but in an even more seamless way. When they're not tabbed, windows are shown as normal without any indication that tabs are even supported, so there's no wasted screen space. But if you want to combine them, you can right-click the title bar in any app and enable stacking for that window, so you can then drag other windows over it to add more tabs to it.

You can then keep adding more apps to that window, or you can just as easily drag one tab out of the window stack to make it into its own window. You can even easily drag a tab from one stacked window to another. There isn't even a visual glitch or anything that really makes the experience feel disjointed or anything, everything happens very smoothly, even in this beta form.

While we're on the topic of window options, there are some other cool options here, like being able to easily turn any window into a "sticky window", meaning it's always on top. You can also take a screenshot of any given window through its context menu.

To tile or not to tile

Easily organize your desktop

Another interesting new feature in COSMIC desktop is how you can manage windows, including built-in support for tiling, but giving the user plenty of control. At any point, you can enable tiling for your current workspace, or switch back to floating mode, as modern desktops tend to work. Turning tiling on means your apps will automatically be added to your screen, splitting your view as needed to fit all your open apps. You can also choose the default behavior for new workspaces, and you can change the behavior for each workspace as you please.

Of course, even if you use floating mode, there are still options for easily snapping windows side by side. You can easily make an app take half the screen, or snap them into quarters for more complex setups. It all works very well. I know some people would like to have a tool that automatically suggests snapping more apps next to your initial one, and that's unfortunately not here. But if you don't mind individually snapping windows into each area, then this is still great.

Theming made easy

Choose your style

Customization is a big aspect of many Linux distros, but COSMIC has its own interesting approach to it, allowing any user to have easy control over what their computer looks like. At any point, you can head into the settings and change the color of various UI elements, including text, backgrounds, and more. You can also choose things like how round corners should be for buttons, or how compact the interface should be in general.

What's even more interesting is that not only can you do this for your own computer, but you can export your settings and share them with the world so they can import them and use your theme easily.

As far as customization goes, COSMIC isn't really on the level of something like KDE, where you can install very elaborate themes that change icons and the like, but there's still a good bit of freedom in this more limited theming engine. It reminds me a lot of Telegram's Android app and how it can be customized in a very similar way, but it's made more approachable here.

Hints of GNOME

COSMIC has some similarities, but it's very different

COSMIC was born because System76 — which had previously relied on GNOME extensions to provide its custom desktop experience for Pop!_OS — had some disagreements with the developers of GNOME. But while COSMIC is still totally independent, it's still very influenced by GNOME, with some elements such as the top menu bar still being present.

However, even for the things that are clearly inspired by GNOME, COSMIC still has some distinctive factors that give it its own flavor, for better or for worse. For example, instead of a single menu in the top right corner with quick buttons for various actions, COSMIC gives you individual applets for various system settings, such as power, networking, Bluetooth, and so on. Each of these provides fairly detailed settings that might otherwise take a few extra clicks in GNOME.

Other differences include the built-in search/launcher, which now opens over the desktop, rather than being part of the workspace overview. By default, you can simply press the Super key, and it automatically launches the keyboard-based launcher rather than opening the applications menu, and you can then start searching for the apps or settings you want right away. It's very fast, too, which means I kind of like it more than KLauncher already.

There are some odd decisions, like the applications menu opening at the top of the screen by default, and some things that appear to be bugs, as you would expect from a beta. I installed COSMIC on top of Arch Linux, and it includes things such as two different icons showing network connectivity information, or an icon that always shows in the dock until I open an app, but it doesn't do anything. These are all things I expect will be polished up before a stable release.

COSMIC is looking great

I haven't set up COSMIC on my main machine yet, but I'm very excited to see how this beta evolves and gets better. I'm still not sure if it can replace KDE Plasma for me, but it does look very promising already, and it could end up joining the ranks as one of the most popular options.

Image credit: System76
COSMIC Desktop
See at Github

Image credit: System76
COSMIC Desktop
See at Github

item.title: Integrated graphics aren’t just for budget PCs; here are 3 other benefits
item.itunes:title: Integrated graphics aren’t just for budget PCs; here are 3 other benefits
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
When we think of a PC with integrated graphics, we're quick to assume that it's a budget system meant for light workloads like web browsing, video streaming, or office work. Sure, depending on the iGPU, you may be able to play some games, but it's not something serious gamers would even consider. However, that doesn't mean there's no reason to have an iGPU in your PC if you already have a discrete graphics card. In fact, it can be surprisingly useful in ways most people overlook.

Even though I have an RTX 4090, I like to think of integrated graphics as a backup that makes my PC more versatile. As someone who currently uses a PC that doesn't have a CPU with integrated graphics, I've realized how inconvenient it can be when my graphics card stops outputting a signal or when it's time to upgrade to a new one. It's one of those things you don't think about until your GPU is faulty and you're troubleshooting issues without a display output. So, let me explain exactly how an iGPU can be beneficial in almost any PC.

iGPU helps save power

Discrete GPUs are not as efficient as iGPUs for basic tasks

You don't need a 300 or 500W GPU firing up every time you browse the web, stream a video, or check your email. Sure, you could argue that discrete GPUs never use anywhere near their full power draw during those light workloads, but the thing is, they're still far less efficient than integrated graphics. A high-end card like my RTX 4090 can easily pull 30 to 50 watts at idle, while an iGPU can handle the same tasks using less than 10 watts.

This difference may not seem huge at first glance, but it can definitely add up over time if you're someone who sits in front of your PC for several hours a day. On laptops, the benefits are even more obvious because they have a direct impact on battery life. That's why modern laptops seamlessly switch between integrated graphics and discrete graphics depending on the workload. While browsing the web or streaming videos, the iGPU takes over to maximize efficiency, allowing the laptop to last longer.

It serves as a backup GPU

A safety net when your GPU dies or you're waiting to upgrade

If you've ever had your graphics card die on you, you probably know how frustrating it can be to have a CPU without integrated graphics. It almost feels like you're helpless when your PC refuses to display anything, and you have no way to even access the BIOS. You'll need a spare GPU in the meantime just to get your system running again, and I'm sure most of you don't have an extra card lying around. That's exactly when an iGPU can be a lifesaver.

The convenience of being able to use your PC while you send your graphics card in for repair or replacement can't be overstated, especially if you rely on it for work. Even if your card hasn't failed, integrated graphics can make GPU upgrades a whole lot easier. For instance, you can sell your old GPU to fund a new one while keeping your PC usable for basic tasks and even some light gaming in the meantime. It’s nice not having any downtime between GPU upgrades, and that's something I only came to appreciate after spending the last five years using CPUs without integrated graphics.

Makes multi-monitor setups easier

Not enough display outputs on your graphics card? No problem

If you've tried connecting more than three monitors to your PC, you've probably run into port limitations since most modern GPUs come with just three DisplayPort outputs. That's exactly when a CPU with integrated graphics can prove to be valuable. You may have already seen a DisplayPort or HDMI port on the back of your motherboard, but that only works if your CPU has an iGPU. Once enabled in the BIOS, you can use those ports alongside your GPU's outputs to connect more monitors.

Of course, if you don't have integrated graphics, you can still use adapters instead of buying a second GPU for more ports, but that often feels like a workaround rather than a proper solution. Some adapters limit refresh rates or cause compatibility issues depending on your monitor setup. And let's not forget about the cable clutter if the adapter needs external power. Even if you do have enough ports, it's better to spread out your displays between your discrete GPU and iGPU. For instance, you can dedicate your graphics card to your primary 4K or high refresh rate monitor while letting integrated graphics handle the secondary 1080p screen.

iGPUs show their worth when you least expect them to

I'll admit that when I built my current PC, the last thing I bothered checking was whether my CPU had integrated graphics. After all, I already had a high-end GPU that could handle anything without breaking a sweat. I didn't think about how handy an iGPU could be until I had to wait for a new graphics card after selling my old one. Without a backup GPU, my PC suddenly felt useless for a month. That's when I realized integrated graphics aren't just for budget builds. They serve as a safety net even in the most powerful rigs. Even if you never use integrated graphics for gaming, you can be sure your PC will remain functional when something goes wrong with your graphics card. And once you've experienced that peace of mind, it's hard to go back to a CPU without an iGPU.

I'll admit that when I built my current PC, the last thing I bothered checking was whether my CPU had integrated graphics. After all, I already had a high-end GPU that could handle anything without breaking a sweat. I didn't think about how handy an iGPU could be until I had to wait for a new graphics card after selling my old one. Without a backup GPU, my PC suddenly felt useless for a month. That's when I realized integrated graphics aren't just for budget builds. They serve as a safety net even in the most powerful rigs. Even if you never use integrated graphics for gaming, you can be sure your PC will remain functional when something goes wrong with your graphics card. And once you've experienced that peace of mind, it's hard to go back to a CPU without an iGPU.
item.content:encoded:
When we think of a PC with integrated graphics, we're quick to assume that it's a budget system meant for light workloads like web browsing, video streaming, or office work. Sure, depending on the iGPU, you may be able to play some games, but it's not something serious gamers would even consider. However, that doesn't mean there's no reason to have an iGPU in your PC if you already have a discrete graphics card. In fact, it can be surprisingly useful in ways most people overlook.

Even though I have an RTX 4090, I like to think of integrated graphics as a backup that makes my PC more versatile. As someone who currently uses a PC that doesn't have a CPU with integrated graphics, I've realized how inconvenient it can be when my graphics card stops outputting a signal or when it's time to upgrade to a new one. It's one of those things you don't think about until your GPU is faulty and you're troubleshooting issues without a display output. So, let me explain exactly how an iGPU can be beneficial in almost any PC.

iGPU helps save power

Discrete GPUs are not as efficient as iGPUs for basic tasks

You don't need a 300 or 500W GPU firing up every time you browse the web, stream a video, or check your email. Sure, you could argue that discrete GPUs never use anywhere near their full power draw during those light workloads, but the thing is, they're still far less efficient than integrated graphics. A high-end card like my RTX 4090 can easily pull 30 to 50 watts at idle, while an iGPU can handle the same tasks using less than 10 watts.

This difference may not seem huge at first glance, but it can definitely add up over time if you're someone who sits in front of your PC for several hours a day. On laptops, the benefits are even more obvious because they have a direct impact on battery life. That's why modern laptops seamlessly switch between integrated graphics and discrete graphics depending on the workload. While browsing the web or streaming videos, the iGPU takes over to maximize efficiency, allowing the laptop to last longer.

It serves as a backup GPU

A safety net when your GPU dies or you're waiting to upgrade

If you've ever had your graphics card die on you, you probably know how frustrating it can be to have a CPU without integrated graphics. It almost feels like you're helpless when your PC refuses to display anything, and you have no way to even access the BIOS. You'll need a spare GPU in the meantime just to get your system running again, and I'm sure most of you don't have an extra card lying around. That's exactly when an iGPU can be a lifesaver.

The convenience of being able to use your PC while you send your graphics card in for repair or replacement can't be overstated, especially if you rely on it for work. Even if your card hasn't failed, integrated graphics can make GPU upgrades a whole lot easier. For instance, you can sell your old GPU to fund a new one while keeping your PC usable for basic tasks and even some light gaming in the meantime. It’s nice not having any downtime between GPU upgrades, and that's something I only came to appreciate after spending the last five years using CPUs without integrated graphics.

Makes multi-monitor setups easier

Not enough display outputs on your graphics card? No problem

If you've tried connecting more than three monitors to your PC, you've probably run into port limitations since most modern GPUs come with just three DisplayPort outputs. That's exactly when a CPU with integrated graphics can prove to be valuable. You may have already seen a DisplayPort or HDMI port on the back of your motherboard, but that only works if your CPU has an iGPU. Once enabled in the BIOS, you can use those ports alongside your GPU's outputs to connect more monitors.

Of course, if you don't have integrated graphics, you can still use adapters instead of buying a second GPU for more ports, but that often feels like a workaround rather than a proper solution. Some adapters limit refresh rates or cause compatibility issues depending on your monitor setup. And let's not forget about the cable clutter if the adapter needs external power. Even if you do have enough ports, it's better to spread out your displays between your discrete GPU and iGPU. For instance, you can dedicate your graphics card to your primary 4K or high refresh rate monitor while letting integrated graphics handle the secondary 1080p screen.

iGPUs show their worth when you least expect them to

I'll admit that when I built my current PC, the last thing I bothered checking was whether my CPU had integrated graphics. After all, I already had a high-end GPU that could handle anything without breaking a sweat. I didn't think about how handy an iGPU could be until I had to wait for a new graphics card after selling my old one. Without a backup GPU, my PC suddenly felt useless for a month. That's when I realized integrated graphics aren't just for budget builds. They serve as a safety net even in the most powerful rigs. Even if you never use integrated graphics for gaming, you can be sure your PC will remain functional when something goes wrong with your graphics card. And once you've experienced that peace of mind, it's hard to go back to a CPU without an iGPU.

item.title: I replaced my iSCSI share with SMB, and I could never go back
item.itunes:title: I replaced my iSCSI share with SMB, and I could never go back
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
For sharing files between machines over a home network, it became essential for me to choose a consistent and relevant storage protocol rather than focusing solely on transfer speeds. Many home labbers prefer iSCSI (Internet Small Computer System Interface) for various tasks. I set it up in my home lab as well, and also used an SMB (Server Message Block). However, I kept going back to using the SMB protocol as it works without issues for my daily workflow and my family as well.

With file sharing at heart, I configured iSCSI shares and SMB shares to make the best of both worlds. The mixed device environment at my home enables me to use devices running Windows, macOS, Linux, Android, iOS, iPadOS, and other media player software. Therefore, I needed to use a storage protocol that supports ease of access on multiple devices, often simultaneously. Once I got used to utilizing the SMB, I couldn’t go back to using iSCSI in my home lab.

Cross-platform compatibility matters the most

Accessible on all devices

One of the primary reasons I prefer SMB is the protocol’s universal compatibility across platforms in my home lab. Like many homes, I use mixed clients carrying different operating systems and form factors. For me, the network setup must be easy and accessible on computers, mobile devices, tablets, smart TVs, and media streaming devices. Almost all operating systems, from Windows to Android, support SMB natively or through file managers.

That relieved me from having to use complicated tools or resort to terminal commands, so that my family and guests could access network shares. Once they can access an SMB share, I am no longer their go-to support guy for browsing media files. For example, it is easy to add SMB shares as media libraries in the Infuse client on Apple TV, allowing me to stream my collection of ripped movies and TV shows. Similarly, I prefer streaming my music collection using Foobar2000 on different clients quickly, thanks to the SMB shares. Compared to that, I struggled to get iSCSI shares working across various clients, except on Windows and Linux.

Easy to set up and tweak for performance

It only takes a few minutes to configure shared folders

Compared to an iSCSI share, I found it easy to configure the SMB server and share. The iSCSI shares required me to specify details for targets, Access Control Lists, and Logical Unit Numbers (LUN). Additionally, it took me some time to ensure that my Windows and Linux machines could both read and write to the iSCSI shares. Since it’s designed primarily for one-to-one connections, setting it up on different devices was tedious.

With SMB shares, I only had to configure the SMB server on my mini PC and the NAS to create shares for large media files and folders, allowing me to upload photos and documents. On Windows and macOS, browsing and viewing an SMB share is quick and easy, mostly depending on how the authentication is configured.

I edited the SMB file server configuration to use the latest protocol and handle more bandwidth, thereby avoiding slowdowns when multiple users access the same file. With some registry tweaks and using Controlled Folder Access on Windows 11, the SMB file server felt zippier. Similarly, I ensured that the SMB file server on Linux used the latest SMB protocol (SMB3) and tweaked its configuration file to accommodate large file transfers.

Supporting multiple clients and multiple users simultaneously

SMB supports concurrent access from several devices

On my NAS, I configured and enabled SMB3 to allow multiple computers to connect simultaneously and access the respective files and folders. While iSCSI delivers solid performance, it is mostly suitable for single client access. I considered configuring a multi-client cluster to connect to iSCSI shares, but decided against it due to the potential risk of data corruption.

Meanwhile, my family and I found it very convenient to access the same file from different devices simultaneously. SMB offers a clean way to assign the necessary permissions so that my family can only browse and view the various files on the media shares. On the SMB shares that they need to upload documents or photos, I can permit them to delete only the ones they added for more granular control.

Flexible and space-efficient backups

Easier options to take backups, restore, and sync

Since SMB provides shares that let me view the file and folder tree, I can select the data to back up or sync between devices. Therefore, timestamps and metadata play a crucial role in this regard. It relieves me from taking a full OS backup, which demands a lot of space. I utilize various backup tools, such as rsync or Duplicati, to work efficiently within the limited storage capacity of my home lab.

At this point, I’m waiting for better HDD deals to set up RAID while using rsync and Duplicati to take proper backups of my documents, photos, and large media files. By teaming RAID 1 with SMB shares, I intend to minimize data loss, even if I accidentally set incorrect permissions.

When I need it the most, SMB works for my home network

I love the speed and efficiency of shared folders across the network. That said, SMB provides the ease of accessing those files and folders using multiple devices by several users. That works for my needs. Of course, SMB works reliably across platforms, is easier to set up, and handles permissions cleanly — it cohesively improves my daily workflow. With files and folders shared across the network, SMB makes it easier to access, view, store, or edit without worrying about the device I use.
